title: An Interview About Urban Planning
summary: A journalist interviews an architect about the challenges and principles of designing liveable, sustainable cities.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Welcome to the programme, Mr Hartley. You've spent over twenty years designing urban spaces. What drew you to this field in the first place?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: Honestly, it was a childhood experience. I grew up in a neighbourhood that had been poorly planned, and I saw first-hand how much the built environment affects people's daily lives.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: Can you give us a sense of what poor urban planning actually looks like on the ground?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: Of course. Think about streets that were designed purely for cars, with no footpaths or shade. Or residential towers that were built without any community spaces — no parks, no shops within walking distance.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: And what happens to communities in those environments?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: Social isolation increases dramatically. Research has shown that if people had been given access to shared green spaces, rates of depression and loneliness would have been significantly lower.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: That's a striking claim. How do architects and planners address those issues today?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: The philosophy has shifted considerably. We now talk about the 'fifteen-minute city' — the idea that every essential service should be reachable on foot or by bicycle within fifteen minutes from home.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: It's a compelling concept. Has it been implemented anywhere successfully?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: Paris is the most celebrated example. The city has been redesigned around this principle over the past decade, and the results have been remarkable in terms of air quality and resident wellbeing.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: What about cities in the developing world, where rapid urbanisation is happening right now?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: That's where the greatest opportunity lies, actually. If new infrastructure were being designed from scratch, planners could embed these principles from the very start rather than retrofitting old systems.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: You mentioned retrofitting. Is it really possible to redesign a city that already exists?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: It's challenging but not impossible. Cities like Medellín in Colombia were once considered ungovernable due to crime, but they were transformed through strategic investment in public transport and public spaces.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: What role does community consultation play in the planning process?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: It's absolutely central. In my experience, projects that were developed without genuine community input were far more likely to fail or create new problems.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: And what's the biggest misconception people have about urban planning?

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: That it's primarily about aesthetics or prestige. In reality, great urban planning is invisible — you don't notice it because everything works. You only notice bad planning when things go wrong.

body.speaker: Interviewer
body.text: That's a fascinating way to put it. Thank you for your time and insights, Mr Hartley.

body.speaker: Architect
body.text: Thank you for having me. I hope these conversations help more people understand how much the spaces we inhabit shape who we become.

questions.prompt: What originally drew Mr Hartley to urban planning?
options: A. A university course he attended
B. A childhood experience of living in a poorly planned neighbourhood
C. A project he worked on early in his career
D. His interest in architecture and aesthetics
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley says: 'I grew up in a neighbourhood that had been poorly planned' — which sparked his interest.
questions.hint: Listen for what he says about his childhood.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is given as an example of poor urban planning?
options: B. Streets designed purely for cars with no footpaths or shade
C. Neighbourhoods with too many parks
D. Office buildings without public transport links
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley mentions streets designed purely for cars with no footpaths as an example of poor planning.
questions.hint: Think about what Mr Hartley says about how streets were designed.

questions.prompt: What third conditional does Mr Hartley use to make a point about mental health?
options: A. If green spaces exist, depression rates fall
B. If people had been given access to green spaces, rates of depression would have been significantly lower
C. If people were given green spaces, they would be happier
D. If cities had parks, mental health would improve
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley uses a third conditional: 'if people had been given access to shared green spaces, rates of depression and loneliness would have been significantly lower.'
questions.hint: He is referring to a past situation that did not happen.

options: A. A city where public transport runs every fifteen minutes
B. A city where all essential services are reachable within fifteen minutes on foot or by bicycle
C. A city that can be built in fifteen years
D. A planning framework that takes fifteen minutes to evaluate
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley explains: 'every essential service should be reachable on foot or by bicycle within fifteen minutes from home.'
questions.hint: Listen to how Mr Hartley defines the concept.

questions.prompt: Which city does Mr Hartley give as the most celebrated example of the fifteen-minute city?
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley says: 'Paris is the most celebrated example.'
questions.hint: He names a specific European capital.

questions.prompt: What passive construction is used to describe Paris's transformation?
options: B. Paris has redesigned its streets
C. The city has been redesigned around this principle
D. Paris was redesigned by its mayor
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley uses the present perfect passive: 'The city has been redesigned around this principle over the past decade.'
questions.hint: He uses a passive form to describe what has happened to Paris.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Hartley say about cities in the developing world?
options: A. They lack the resources to implement good planning
B. They represent the greatest opportunity to embed good principles from the start
C. They should copy European models exactly
D. They are already ahead of Western cities in planning
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley says: 'That's where the greatest opportunity lies' because infrastructure could be designed from scratch.
questions.hint: He is optimistic about these cities for a specific reason.

questions.prompt: What second conditional does Mr Hartley use about new infrastructure?
options: A. If new infrastructure is designed from scratch, planners will embed principles
B. If new infrastructure were being designed from scratch, planners could embed these principles from the very start
C. If new infrastructure had been designed from scratch, planners would have embedded principles
D. If new infrastructure were designed, principles could be embedded
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley uses a second conditional: 'If new infrastructure were being designed from scratch, planners could embed these principles from the very start.'
questions.hint: He is describing a hypothetical present/ongoing situation.

questions.prompt: Which city does Mr Hartley use to demonstrate that existing cities can be transformed?
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley mentions Medellín in Colombia as a city once considered ungovernable that was transformed.
questions.hint: He names a South American city known for its dramatic turnaround.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Hartley say about projects developed without community input?
options: A. They tend to be more cost-effective
B. They are often completed more quickly
C. They were far more likely to fail or create new problems
D. They receive less media attention
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley says: 'projects that were developed without genuine community input were far more likely to fail or create new problems.'
questions.hint: He uses a passive construction to make this point.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Hartley say is the biggest misconception about urban planning?
options: A. That it is too expensive
B. That it only benefits wealthy residents
C. That it is primarily about aesthetics or prestige
D. That it can only be done by governments
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley states that the biggest misconception is 'that it's primarily about aesthetics or prestige.'
questions.hint: He corrects a common misunderstanding about what planning is for.

questions.prompt: According to Mr Hartley, when do you notice urban planning?
options: A. When a new building is opened
B. When good design is celebrated publicly
C. Only when things go wrong
D. When the city wins international awards
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley says: 'You only notice bad planning when things go wrong.'
questions.hint: He makes a point about the invisibility of good planning.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Hartley say in his closing remark about the spaces we inhabit?
options: A. They reflect our economic status
B. They shape who we become
C. They should be designed by residents
D. They define a city's cultural identity
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley closes with: 'the spaces we inhabit shape who we become.'
questions.hint: Listen to his final philosophical statement.

questions.prompt: What is the overall tone of the interview?
questions.explanation: The interviewer asks open questions and Mr Hartley provides reflective, evidence-based answers, making the tone informative and thoughtful.
questions.hint: Consider both the format and the language used throughout.

questions.prompt: What word describes the approach planners use when updating old cities rather than building new ones?
questions.explanation: Mr Hartley uses the word 'retrofitting' to describe adapting existing infrastructure.
questions.hint: He uses a specific technical term that the interviewer picks up on.

cloze.template: In the interview, Mr Hartley explained that he became interested in urban planning because he grew up in a neighbourhood that had been poorly {{b1}}. He gave examples of poor planning, including streets that were {{b2}} purely for cars with no footpaths or shade. He argued that if people had been {{b3}} access to shared green spaces, rates of depression would have been significantly {{b4}}. Today, planners talk about the '{{b5}}-minute city', the idea that essential services should be {{b6}} on foot within fifteen minutes. Paris was described as the most {{b7}} example, having been {{b8}} around this principle over the past decade. For cities in the developing world, Mr Hartley said the greatest {{b9}} lies in designing new infrastructure from {{b10}}, embedding good principles from the start rather than {{b11}} old systems. He also stressed that community {{b12}} is central to the planning process, noting that projects {{b13}} without genuine community input were far more likely to fail. Finally, he argued that great urban planning is {{b14}} — you only notice bad planning when things go {{b15}}.

blanks.explanation: Mr Hartley says he grew up in a neighbourhood 'that had been poorly planned'.
The passage says 'streets that were designed purely for cars' — passive voice, simple past.
Third conditional passive: 'if people had been given access to shared green spaces'.
The conditional result: 'rates of depression and loneliness would have been significantly lower'.
Services 'should be reachable on foot or by bicycle within fifteen minutes'.
Mr Hartley calls Paris 'the most celebrated example'.
The passive: 'The city has been redesigned around this principle'.
Mr Hartley says: 'That's where the greatest opportunity lies'.
He uses the phrase 'designed from scratch' — meaning starting completely fresh.
Mr Hartley contrasts embedding principles 'from the start' versus 'retrofitting old systems'.
The interviewer asks about 'community consultation' and Mr Hartley says it is 'absolutely central'.
'Projects that were developed without genuine community input' — passive relative clause.
Mr Hartley says: 'great urban planning is invisible — you don't notice it because everything works'.
'You only notice bad planning when things go wrong.'

annotations.meaningVi: trực tiếp / từ kinh nghiệm bản thân
annotations.exampleEn: She witnessed first-hand how poverty affects children's education.

annotations.meaningVi: môi trường xây dựng (không gian do con người tạo ra)
annotations.exampleEn: Researchers study how the built environment influences public health outcomes.

annotations.meaningVi: sự cô lập xã hội
annotations.exampleEn: Urban design that lacks communal spaces can contribute to social isolation.

annotations.meaningVi: thành phố mười lăm phút
annotations.exampleEn: The fifteen-minute city concept has influenced urban policy in several European capitals.

annotations.meaningVi: có thể đến được
annotations.exampleEn: The station is reachable on foot in under ten minutes from the city centre.

annotations.meaningVi: đô thị hóa nhanh chóng
annotations.exampleEn: Rapid urbanisation in Southeast Asia is creating pressure on infrastructure and housing.

annotations.meaningVi: từ đầu / từ đầu không có gì
annotations.exampleEn: The team rebuilt the entire system from scratch after the old one failed.

annotations.meaningVi: cải tạo / nâng cấp hệ thống cũ
annotations.exampleEn: Retrofitting older buildings with better insulation can reduce energy consumption significantly.

annotations.meaningVi: không thể quản lý / hỗn loạn
annotations.exampleEn: The city had once been considered ungovernable, but reforms turned it around.

annotations.meaningVi: đầu tư có chiến lược
annotations.exampleEn: Strategic investment in public transport transformed the city's mobility.

annotations.meaningVi: tham vấn cộng đồng
annotations.exampleEn: Community consultation was essential before the new park was approved.

annotations.meaningVi: quan niệm sai lầm
annotations.exampleEn: There is a common misconception that good design is only about appearance.

annotations.meaningVi: tính thẩm mỹ
annotations.exampleEn: The architect was praised for balancing aesthetics with practicality.

annotations.meaningVi: uy tín / danh tiếng / sự sang trọng
annotations.exampleEn: The tower was built more for prestige than for the needs of residents.

annotations.meaningVi: vô hình / không nhận ra được
annotations.exampleEn: Good infrastructure is invisible — you only miss it when it breaks down.

annotations.meaningVi: tích hợp / cài vào
annotations.exampleEn: Sustainability was embedded in the design from the very beginning.

annotations.meaningVi: sức khoẻ và hạnh phúc của cư dân
annotations.exampleEn: City policies increasingly prioritise resident wellbeing over economic growth.

annotations.meaningVi: khái niệm thuyết phục / hấp dẫn
annotations.exampleEn: The fifteen-minute city is a compelling concept that is gaining global traction.

annotations.meaningVi: ý kiến đóng góp thực sự từ cộng đồng
annotations.exampleEn: Without genuine community input, the project risked being rejected by residents.

annotations.meaningVi: sinh sống / cư trú trong
annotations.exampleEn: The spaces we inhabit profoundly influence our sense of identity and belonging.

grammarNotes.title: Second and Third Conditionals — Contrasting Hypotheticals
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài nghe sử dụng cả câu điều kiện loại 2 và loại 3 để so sánh các tình huống giả định. Loại 2 (If + V2, would + V): đề cập đến tình huống không thực trong hiện tại hoặc tương lai. Ví dụ: 'If new infrastructure were being designed from scratch, planners could embed these principles.' Loại 3 (If + had + V3, would have + V3): đề cập đến tình huống không thực trong quá khứ. Ví dụ: 'If people had been given access to green spaces, rates of depression would have been significantly lower.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses both second and third conditionals to discuss hypothetical urban scenarios. Second conditional (If + past simple, would + infinitive): imagines an unreal present or future. Example: 'If new infrastructure were being designed from scratch, planners could embed these principles from the very start.' Third conditional (If + past perfect, would have + past participle): imagines a different past. Example: 'If people had been given access to green spaces, rates of depression would have been significantly lower.' Notice that both examples also use the passive voice.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice in Describing Urban Change
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thể bị động xuất hiện nhiều trong bài vì nó phù hợp khi nói về các quá trình thành phố trải qua theo thời gian mà không cần xác định chủ thể cụ thể. Ví dụ: 'The city has been redesigned' (present perfect passive), 'streets were designed purely for cars' (simple past passive), 'projects were developed without community input' (simple past passive). Khi cần nhấn mạnh tác nhân, dùng 'by': 'transformed by strategic investment'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Passive voice is especially common in discussions of urban planning because the focus is on what happened to places rather than who did it. Key passive structures in this lesson: present perfect passive — 'The city has been redesigned around this principle'; simple past passive — 'streets were designed purely for cars'; passive in a relative clause — 'projects that were developed without genuine community input.' When the agent matters, it is introduced with 'by': 'they were transformed through strategic investment.'

translationVi: Trong cuộc phỏng vấn, kiến trúc sư Hartley giải thích rằng ông quan tâm đến quy hoạch đô thị vì ông lớn lên trong một khu phố được quy hoạch kém và tận mắt chứng kiến môi trường xây dựng ảnh hưởng đến cuộc sống hàng ngày của con người như thế nào. Ông đưa ra ví dụ về những con đường được thiết kế hoàn toàn cho xe hơi mà không có vỉa hè hay bóng mát, và các tòa nhà dân cư được xây dựng mà không có không gian cộng đồng. Ông lập luận rằng nếu mọi người được tiếp cận các không gian xanh chung, tỷ lệ trầm cảm sẽ thấp hơn đáng kể. Ngày nay, các nhà quy hoạch nói về 'thành phố mười lăm phút', trong đó mọi dịch vụ thiết yếu có thể đến được bằng cách đi bộ hoặc xe đạp trong vòng mười lăm phút. Paris được nêu là ví dụ tiêu biểu nhất. Ông cũng nhấn mạnh rằng các dự án được phát triển mà không có sự tham gia thực sự của cộng đồng thường có nhiều khả năng thất bại hơn. Cuối cùng, ông nói rằng quy hoạch đô thị tốt là vô hình — bạn chỉ nhận thấy quy hoạch kém khi mọi thứ đi sai.
criticalThinkingQuestion: Mr Hartley argues that the 'fifteen-minute city' model improves quality of life and mental health. What potential drawbacks or unintended consequences might arise if this model were applied universally, particularly in cities with very different social, economic, or cultural contexts?
